title: PyEntrez
description: Python wrapper for NCBI's EDirect command-line tools. Provides a programmatic API for searching PubMed and other NCBI databases, fetching records, discovering cross-database links, pulling citation counts, and querying by MeSH terms. Includes batch processing with parallel workers for bulk operations.

blog.subtitle: A thin wrapper that does exactly one thing correctly

sections.heading: Why wrap EDirect instead of calling the API directly
paragraphs: NCBI publishes E-utilities, a direct HTTP API for searching PubMed and fetching records. It also ships EDirect, a set of command-line tools that wrap E-utilities with batching, chunking, and retry logic already built in. PyEntrez wraps EDirect rather than calling E-utilities directly because EDirect has years of production handling of NCBI's edge cases: malformed XML responses, partial fetches, rate limit responses that look like server errors. Writing those handlers from scratch against the raw API means reimplementing work that already exists and is already correct.

The problem with EDirect in a Python context is the shell pipe model. Subprocesses block, broken pipes disappear without raising, and non-zero exits are silently discarded if you're not explicitly checking. In a Jupyter notebook or any concurrent Python script, that model is unreliable in ways that are hard to debug.

sections.heading: Subprocess design
paragraphs: Each EDirect tool is invoked separately via `subprocess.Popen`. The output of one is read into Python, parsed, and passed to the next tool as stdin, so non-zero exits raise Python exceptions with the actual NCBI response attached rather than disappearing into the pipe. Jupyter was still blocking on subprocess stdout reads until the IO was moved to a background thread, which unblocked the event loop. The rule is that it fails loudly: no silent swallowing of errors, no retries that hide the original failure.

sections.heading: Rate limiting
paragraphs: NCBI allows three requests per second unauthenticated and ten with an API key, and exceeding that rate results in a message from an actual human at NCBI. The batch worker uses a token-bucket limiter shared across threads, refilling at the rate NCBI permits, where any worker that needs a token and can't get one blocks until one is available. No retries, no exponential backoff, just wait your turn.

sections.heading: Streaming XML
paragraphs: The bulk download path uses `lxml` iterparse rather than loading a full articleset XML file into memory. Each article element is yielded as it parses and written to its own file, which lets the next stage fan out across processes instead of walking a large file serially. This matters at the scale of the Open Access subset, where the files are large enough that a DOM parser exhausts available memory.

sections.heading: Scope
paragraphs: PyEntrez exposes what EDirect exposes and nothing more. It doesn't add search ranking, result filtering, or any logic that tries to interpret what NCBI returns. That scope is a deliberate design decision: a library that adds behavior on top of an API has to track every API change that affects that behavior, and NCBI changes its response formats more often than its interfaces. Keeping the wrapper thin means it stays correct without maintenance.
